notes: BG 2.10 is pure narration (Sanjaya describing Krishna's settled-teacher posture before he begins to speak at 2.11). The doctrinally significant element — prahasann iva (smiling 'as if'), where the iva qualifies the smile and distinguishes it from amusement — is preserved precisely by the engineering layer: 'smiling \"as if\" — not amused, but settled.' This honors the commentarial reading (Shankara explicitly notes the iva is meaningful — 'not a smile of amusement, a smile of teacher's settled readiness'). The geometry of the moment (senayor ubhayor madhye — between the two armies) is preserved in the analog ('the two armies of the room — the team that wanted the migration, the team that opposed it — are still arranged on either side of the issue'). The engineering layer correctly identifies the verse's role as the structural pivot before the teaching at 2.11 ('this verse merely names the pivot moment between the operator's exhausted silence and the teacher's settled reply') — which mirrors the commentarial consensus. The verse does not invert any Sanskrit claim; it correctly refuses to manufacture a teaching from the smile. PASS.
